## [12:05 - 15:08]

We'll use an international SWIFT code if we're working with international clients. Bank transfers are our preference. However, sometimes that can be difficult with some clients, so we use Stripe. Profit and loss is very simple, but it's a fundamental concept in your business, otherwise known as a P&L. Essentially what this does is it allows you to get a really good picture if your business is going to be profitable or not, and what revenue targets you need to hit to achieve certain outcomes that you want in your business. The three main things that goes into a profit and loss is your expected revenues and your expenses, and then the profit margin. This document essentially shows you the profit and the loss that you're going to make in your business. It's a budgeting tool as well, meaning it's forecasted. You are forecasting these revenues, and you're going to forecast these expenses, and then you're going to determine that profit margin that you want. It's going to tell you whether or not that business is going to be profitable or not. I'm going to take you into zero and show you an example of how this could be done. This is a dummy zero account here. This is our invoicing software. I think QuickBooks has this as well. What we're going to do is click on reports, and we're going to start off going into budget manager. Now this is going to be different, obviously, depending on the invoicing or accounting software you use. You can even do this on a spreadsheet, guys. This is not rocket science here. What we've got here is income. What we'll do is, for example, if we're anticipating $20,000, and you might cross that into the next few months, then expenses. Cost of sales now, we're lucky because our cost of goods is not that high here. So operating expenses, advertising, so let's say we're spending $3,000 on Facebook. You might decide to bring that over. Bank fees, cleaning, it's probably more like $400 for me. Consulting and accounting. So let's say we've got consultants working with us. Depreciation, so talk to your accountant about that. Entertainment, are you buying drinks for your team every week? Entertainment, non-deductible insurance, so you get the gist. So you basically go through, and this is amazing because this is a lot of stuff that you don't think about. If you've been running a business for a while through an accounting software, zero, for example, we'll pull out a lot of these figures automatically.

Now let's say we had budgeted for 100,000, because obviously you're not going to be spending 54K on staff if you're not actually generating, hopefully, that much money. So you'll see here our net profit is 26. Now we want to have a profit margin of 20 to 30%. The average in the agency industry, you can aim for a lot higher, but obviously that's ideal, so it will go 26,165, whoops, divide it by 100,000. And our profit margin at the moment is 26%. So that may or may not be acceptable. Maybe we need a 30% profit margin, which means we actually need to earn more money, or we need to cut our costs down. So this is really, really important, so you need to make sure you're protecting a profit margin that goes in your business. For those of you that are not sure why, a profit margin essentially, let me go over here. I think I've gone backwards. Profit margin essentially allows you to reinvest and to grow and to scale, and to also have that in your business in case anything happens. It is not okay that you take all the money out of your business, that you bank everything. So let's say you've got $40,000 in revenue, it's not okay that you take out $40,000 to pay yourself after all your expenses. You need to make sure you have a profit margin in your business so that you can pay for people to come in and set up CRMs for you, contractors if you need it, consultants if you ever need it, reinvestment, hiring someone so you want to make sure that you have enough for their income ideally, right? Their salary, sorry. And if anything ever happens, like you lose a client, it means that you're not going to be going bankrupt and having to shut down your agency because you guys can't support

## [18:09 - 19:37]

yourself for a month. The only situation where that's okay is if you actually have to take out all the money just to pay your personal expenses. If you're fine, if you've covered your mortgage and the minimums that you need to live your life, then leave a margin in your business and that should be 20 to 30%. We operate out of 30%. So if we made $100,000, $30,000 needs to stay back in the business.
